title: Should a town keep the public clock that has always run slow?
summary: Argue whether a beloved but inaccurate town clock should be repaired to perfect time or left to keep the wrong time everyone has learned to live by.
topic: Whether a public clock that runs slightly wrong should be corrected or preserved as the shared, imperfect time of a place
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on whether a town should repair its old public clock — which has run four minutes slow for as long as anyone remembers — or leave it inaccurate. Argue what a shared 'wrong' time gives a community that a perfectly accurate one cannot, and whether keeping an error is preservation or mere sentimentality. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and concession markers.
hintStarters: What a town loses when its clock is finally corrected is not accuracy but…
Granted, a clock that lies about the time is still, by any technical measure…
It is not the right time that a public clock keeps, but the time a place has agreed to…
We assume that fixing an error always improves a thing, when sometimes the error has become…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nét lập dị / đặc điểm riêng
di sản / cái được thừa hưởng
nét riêng kỳ lạ / đặc dị
đại tu / sửa chữa toàn diện
thuộc về công dân / đô thị

sampleText:
The clock on the old market hall in a small Saxon town has run four minutes slow since before the oldest residents were born. Everyone knows this. Mothers tell children to be home by 'the hall clock', which means four minutes later than the phones say. The buses, run from the city, ignore it; the town does not. When a heritage grant arrived to restore the mechanism, it came with an obvious assumption — that 'restore' meant 'make accurate' — and the town, to the surprise of the engineers, was not sure it agreed.

What a town loses when its clock is finally corrected is not accuracy but a kind of shared agreement, one that no app can supply. A phone tells each person the time privately, in their own pocket; the hall clock tells everyone the same time at once, out loud, in the square. That the time is slightly wrong is precisely what makes it the town's own. It is not the right time that a public clock keeps, but the time a place has agreed to live by — and an agreement, unlike a measurement, belongs to the people who hold it.

Consider what 'on hall-clock time' has come to mean. A whole town has organised its small mercies around four minutes: the late riser forgiven, the market trader granted a moment to lay out his stall, the latecomer to a funeral who slips in unremarked. The error has become an inheritance, passed down without anyone teaching it. Or consider the lighthouse keepers of a Breton island who, for generations, set their watches to a bell that rang a few minutes early so that no boat would ever leave too late; the inaccuracy was not a fault but a margin, built deliberately into the day. Correct it, and you have not fixed a problem. You have erased a habit of care.

Granted, a clock that lies about the time is still, by any technical measure, broken, and there is something indulgent about defending an error simply because it is old. Sentimentality can dress up any failure as character. A visitor who misses a train because they trusted the square would be right to feel the romance had cost them something real, and a town cannot run its hospitals or its railways on a clock that is fond rather than correct. The objection has weight, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest.

Even so, no one is proposing that ambulances be dispatched by the market hall. The accurate time is everywhere, on every wrist and screen, freely available to anyone who needs it. We assume that fixing an error always improves a thing, when sometimes the error has become the thing — the one clock in a person's life that is not synchronised to a distant server, that runs on local agreement rather than satellite, and that a community has made its own precisely by living around its flaw.

The case for leaving the clock slow is not a case against accuracy. It is a case for letting a place keep one shared object that answers to it alone. A town that corrects its clock gains four minutes it never lacked and loses something it cannot get back: the small, communal pride of telling the time in a way that is theirs, and wrong, and entirely agreed upon.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to redefine what a public clock is for
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is not the right time that a public clock keeps, but the time a place has agreed to live by' dùng câu chẻ phủ định it-cleft để định nghĩa lại chức năng của đồng hồ: không phải đo giờ đúng mà là giữ một thỏa thuận chung.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is not the right time that a public clock keeps, but the time a place has agreed to live by' uses a negative it-cleft to redefine the clock's purpose: not measuring correct time but holding a shared agreement.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to depersonalise the act of correction
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'when its clock is finally corrected', 'built deliberately into the day', và 'ambulances be dispatched' dùng bị động để biến việc chỉnh đồng hồ thành một hành động chung, khách quan, tách khỏi bất kỳ cá nhân nào.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'when its clock is finally corrected', 'built deliberately into the day', and 'ambulances be dispatched' use the passive to treat correction as a collective, impersonal act rather than any one person's choice.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chiếc đồng hồ trên nhà hội chợ cũ ở một thị trấn nhỏ vùng Saxony đã chạy chậm bốn phút từ trước khi những cư dân già nhất ra đời. Ai cũng biết điều này. Các bà mẹ dặn con về nhà trước giờ 'đồng hồ nhà hội', tức là muộn hơn bốn phút so với điện thoại báo. Các chuyến xe buýt, do thành phố vận hành, phớt lờ nó; thị trấn thì không. Khi một khoản tài trợ di sản đến để phục chế bộ máy, nó đi kèm một giả định hiển nhiên — rằng 'phục chế' có nghĩa là 'làm cho chính xác' — và thị trấn, trước sự ngạc nhiên của các kỹ sư, không chắc mình đồng ý.

Cái một thị trấn đánh mất khi đồng hồ của nó cuối cùng được chỉnh đúng không phải là sự chính xác mà là một kiểu thỏa thuận chung, thứ mà không ứng dụng nào có thể cung cấp. Một chiếc điện thoại báo cho từng người giờ giấc một cách riêng tư, trong túi của riêng họ; đồng hồ nhà hội báo cho mọi người cùng một giờ một lúc, vang ra, ngay giữa quảng trường. Việc giờ giấc hơi sai lại chính là điều khiến nó trở thành của riêng thị trấn. Cái mà một đồng hồ công cộng giữ không phải là giờ đúng, mà là giờ mà một nơi đã đồng ý sống theo — và một thỏa thuận, khác với một phép đo, thuộc về những người nắm giữ nó.

Hãy xét xem 'theo giờ đồng hồ nhà hội' đã dần có nghĩa gì. Cả một thị trấn đã sắp xếp những ân huệ nhỏ của mình quanh bốn phút ấy: người dậy muộn được tha thứ, người buôn chợ được cho một khoảnh khắc để bày sạp hàng, kẻ đến muộn một đám tang lặng lẽ len vào mà không bị để ý. Cái sai đã trở thành một di sản, được truyền lại mà không ai dạy nó. Hoặc hãy xét đến những người gác hải đăng của một hòn đảo vùng Breton, những người, qua bao thế hệ, chỉnh đồng hồ của họ theo một tiếng chuông rung sớm vài phút để không con thuyền nào rời đi quá trễ; sự thiếu chính xác không phải là một lỗi mà là một khoảng dư, được dựng vào ngày một cách có chủ ý. Hãy chỉnh nó cho đúng, và bạn không hề sửa được một vấn đề. Bạn đã xóa đi một thói quen quan tâm.

Công nhận, một đồng hồ nói dối về giờ giấc thì vẫn, theo bất kỳ thước đo kỹ thuật nào, là hỏng, và có điều gì đó nuông chiều trong việc bảo vệ một cái lỗi chỉ vì nó cũ. Tính ủy mị có thể khoác cho bất kỳ thất bại nào cái áo của cá tính. Một du khách lỡ chuyến tàu vì đã tin vào quảng trường sẽ có lý khi thấy cái lãng mạn ấy đã khiến họ mất đi một thứ có thật, và một thị trấn không thể vận hành bệnh viện hay đường sắt của mình bằng một đồng hồ trìu mến thay vì chính xác. Phản đối này có trọng lượng, và giả vờ ngược lại thì sẽ là thiếu trung thực.

Dẫu vậy, không ai đề xuất rằng xe cứu thương phải được điều động theo nhà hội chợ. Giờ đúng có ở khắp nơi, trên mọi cổ tay và màn hình, sẵn có miễn phí cho bất cứ ai cần đến. Chúng ta cho rằng sửa một cái lỗi thì luôn cải thiện một thứ, trong khi đôi khi cái lỗi đã trở thành chính cái thứ ấy — chiếc đồng hồ duy nhất trong đời một người không được đồng bộ với một máy chủ xa xôi, thứ chạy theo thỏa thuận địa phương thay vì vệ tinh, và mà một cộng đồng đã biến thành của riêng mình chính bằng cách sống quanh khuyết điểm của nó.

Lý lẽ cho việc để đồng hồ chạy chậm không phải là lý lẽ chống lại sự chính xác. Đó là lý lẽ cho việc để một nơi giữ lại một vật chung chỉ chịu trách nhiệm trước riêng nó. Một thị trấn chỉnh lại đồng hồ của mình có được bốn phút mà nó chưa bao giờ thiếu và đánh mất một thứ nó không thể lấy lại: niềm tự hào nhỏ bé, mang tính cộng đồng của việc xem giờ theo một cách là của họ, và sai, và hoàn toàn được nhất trí.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a negative it-cleft to redefine the purpose of a public clock?
options: A public clock keeps it is not the right time the place has agreed by to live but.
It is not the right time that a public clock keeps, but the time a place has agreed to live by.
The time a place has agreed it is not what a public clock to live by keeps right.
Live by the time a place agreed it is not keeps right a public clock but.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is not X … but Y' is a negative it-cleft that contrasts correct time with agreed time.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is not … that … , but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to treat correction as an impersonal, collective act?
options: The mayor personally walks up and changes the clock himself each year.
Somebody specific definitely chose to correct the clock on purpose.
What a town loses when its clock is finally corrected is not accuracy but a kind of shared agreement.
Corrected is the clock when a town its accuracy loses finally.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'when its clock is finally corrected' uses the passive, leaving the corrector unnamed.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is + past participle' without naming who acts?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the counter-argument most fairly?
options: Obviously only fools would ever fix an old clock.
Of course accuracy never matters to anyone anywhere.
Naturally, anyone who wants the right time is a soulless bureaucrat.
Granted, a clock that lies about the time is still, by any technical measure, broken.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' honestly admits the clock is, technically, broken.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the writer's preferred clock is genuinely inaccurate?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a convention' mean as used here?
options: A shared agreement or custom that a group lives by
A large meeting or conference
A mechanical clock part
An official government law
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A convention' is a shared custom — the agreed, communal time the essay defends.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of an agreement a community holds in common.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the concession back to the argument?
options: In conclusion, accuracy is the enemy of all communities.
Even so, no one is proposing that ambulances be dispatched by the market hall.
On the contrary, hospitals should run on the slow clock.
Anyway, time itself is not real.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession about safety and limits the argument's scope sensibly.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase grants the prior point and narrows the claim?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: Honestly the old clock is just so cute and they should keep it, right?
Basically fixing the clock would totally ruin the whole vibe, period.
That the time is slightly wrong is precisely what makes it the town's own.
It kind of warms my heart that the clock is wrong, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and analytical; the others slip into casual or sentimental speech.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'vibe', and 'kind of warms my heart'.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does a town actually lose by correcting the clock?
options: Four minutes of accuracy it badly needed
Its connection to the satellite network
The trust of the bus company
A shared agreement no app can supply
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues the loss is the communal agreement, not accuracy, which is available everywhere.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the second paragraph's central claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: the late riser forgiven, the market trader granted a moment, the latecomer who slips in unremarked
forgiving the late riser, the trader to grant a moment, and slipping in the latecomer
the late riser to forgive, granting the trader, and the latecomer slips unremarked in who
forgive the riser late, the moment granted trader, and unremarked slips latecomer the
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched noun phrases with past-participle modifiers form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three items in the same form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the example of the Breton lighthouse bell illustrate?
options: That all clocks should be fast
That an inaccuracy can be a deliberate margin built in as a habit of care
That islands cannot keep accurate time
That bells are more reliable than clocks
mcQuestions.explanation: The bell rang early on purpose, so the 'error' was a built-in safety margin, not a fault.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread why the bell rang a few minutes early.

options: Careful technical reasoning
A type of clock mechanism
An excessive, soft attachment that can excuse real failings
Strict insistence on accuracy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Sentimentality' is over-soft feeling that, the essay admits, 'can dress up any failure as character.'
mcQuestions.hint: Think of feeling that excuses a fault by calling it charm.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a semicolon to join two contrasting clauses?
options: The buses run from; the city ignore it the town does not.
The buses run from the city; ignore it the town; does not.
The buses run; from the city ignore it the town does not;
The buses, run from the city, ignore it; the town does not.
mcQuestions.explanation: A semicolon correctly joins two complete, contrasting clauses.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places the semicolon between two full sentences?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's final position?
options: Leaving the clock slow lets a place keep one shared object that answers to it alone
Accuracy should always be sacrificed for tradition
The clock should be replaced with a digital screen
No town should ever own a public clock
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for preserving the clock as a uniquely communal, self-governed object, not against accuracy as such.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the closing distinction in the final paragraph.

options: A widely shared standard
A distinctive peculiarity unique to one person or thing
A mechanical breakdown
An official measurement
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Idiosyncrasy' is a peculiar individual trait — like the clock's four-minute quirk.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a quirk that belongs to just one thing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's central reframing?
options: The clock is broken and must be thrown away.
Every error in the world should be preserved forever.
We assume that fixing an error always improves a thing, when sometimes the error has become the thing.
Accurate clocks have no place in any town.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the essay's key reframing: the flaw has become the very identity worth keeping.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option says the error itself has 'become the thing'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps the concession honest rather than dismissive?
options: Anyone who wants accurate time is just a joyless machine.
Missing a train because of the clock is always the visitor's own fault.
No real cost ever comes from a slow clock, ever.
A visitor who misses a train because they trusted the square would be right to feel the romance had cost them something real.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B genuinely grants that the inaccuracy can harm an outsider, keeping the argument fair.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits a real cost to the slow clock?

options: Shared by and belonging to a whole community
Belonging to one private individual
Strictly accurate and official
Recently built or installed
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Communal' means held in common by a community — the kind of pride the clock gives.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of something owned collectively by a group.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a shared 'error' in your own community or family — a tradition, a nickname, a rule that everyone knows is technically wrong but keeps anyway? What would actually be lost if someone insisted on correcting it, and who would feel that loss most?
